title: The Endless Final Stage
tags: Spark job stuck at 99% because of data skew
businessContext: You own a daily Spark ETL that joins a very large clickstream_events table with a user_profiles table to build a personalization layer for dashboards.
problemStatement: The job reaches 99 percent quickly, 199 out of 200 tasks finish, but one task runs for hours with high GC and 100 percent CPU before ending with OutOfMemoryError.
requirement: Fix the PySpark code so the pipeline is correct, scalable, and safe to rerun.
schema: DataFrames depend on the scenario. Assume large production-scale inputs, skewed keys, retries, and partitioned lake storage.
sampleInput: Use the code comments and logs to infer the input shape. Focus on the production failure mode, not local toy execution.
brokenCode:
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

events = spark.read.parquet(clickstream_path)
profiles = spark.read.parquet(user_profiles_path)

# Broken: one hot/null user_id can push most rows into one shuffle task.
result = (events
  .join(profiles, on='user_id', how='left')
  .groupBy('user_id')
  .agg(F.count('*').alias('event_count')))

result.write.mode('overwrite').parquet(output_path)
expectedOutput: Corrected PySpark code or approach should reduce the failure mode, preserve correctness, and include validation/monitoring.
logs:
[Spark] The Endless Final Stage
Stage progress: most tasks finished, one or more tasks are long-running.
Metrics to inspect: shuffle read, spill, skew ratio, executor lost count, file count.
The job reaches 99 percent quickly, 199 out of 200 tasks finish, but one task runs for hours with
high GC and 100 percent CPU before ending with OutOfMemoryError.
hints: Whether you can diagnose skew from execution symptoms instead of randomly tuning configs.
A few keys dominate the join or aggregation workload.
Confirm the hypothesis in the Spark UI by comparing task duration, input size, and shuffle read size;
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix the broken PySpark code sample.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: Fix the PySpark pipeline by addressing the root production issue, not just adding resources. • Confirm the hypothesis in the Spark UI by comparing task duration, input size, and shuffle read size; one task should be a clear outlier. • Profile the skewed keys using approximate counts on the join key and inspect null/default buckets. • Replace groupByKey with reduceByKey or map-side aggregation where possible. • Use salting, skew hints, or split hot keys into dedicated handling logic. • Broadcast only when the smaller side truly fits safely in executor memory and stays stable across retries.
productionExplanation: This is usually not a generic 'Spark is slow' problem. One partition is much heavier than others, often because a hot key pulls too much data into one task during shuffle. Trade-offs: • Salting adds code complexity and requires desalinizing later. • Broadcasting helps only when one side is truly small; a wrong broadcast can create new memory failures. • Adding more partitions can improve balance but also increase scheduler and shuffle overhead. Monitoring and testing: • Track task duration percentiles, skew ratio between max and median task time, shuffle read size by task, and GC time. • Add data-quality checks for unexpected hot keys, null key share, and distribution drift on the join column. • Load test the pipeline with known skewed samples before release.
commonMistakes: A few keys dominate the join or aggregation workload.
groupByKey forces large value lists to one reducer instead of pre-aggregating.
Default partitioning creates one oversized shuffle partition.
The join key has nulls, defaults, or low-cardinality values that collapse many rows together.
followUps: How would you decide between salting and broadcast?
Why is groupByKey dangerous here?
What exact Spark UI metrics would you open first?

title: The Executor Graveyard
tags: Repeated executor deaths after a wide join
businessContext: A customer 360 pipeline joins multiple large fact-like datasets and enriches them with dimensions before writing curated Parquet output.
problemStatement: The job does not freeze at one task; instead executors keep dying and getting replaced. Retries happen repeatedly and the stage eventually fails.

source_df = spark.read.parquet(source_path)

# Broken: this code is functionally plausible but unsafe for production scale/reruns.
result_df = (source_df
  .join(reference_df, on='id', how='left')
  .groupBy('id')
  .agg(F.count('*').alias('record_count')))

result_df.write.mode('append').parquet(output_path)
expectedOutput: Corrected PySpark code or approach should reduce the failure mode, preserve correctness, and include validation/monitoring.
logs:
[Spark] The Executor Graveyard
Stage progress: most tasks finished, one or more tasks are long-running.
Metrics to inspect: shuffle read, spill, skew ratio, executor lost count, file count.
The job does not freeze at one task; instead executors keep dying and getting replaced. Retries
happen repeatedly and the stage eventually fails.
hints: Whether you can separate memory-pressure issues from skew issues and propose practical remediations.
Shuffle partitions are too large because the cluster or input size changed but partition counts did not.

tasks: Diagnose the issue.

modelSolution: Fix the PySpark pipeline by addressing the root production issue, not just adding resources. • Inspect the physical plan and stage metrics to see where the data blows up. • Push filters and projection earlier so fewer columns and rows enter the heavy join. • Revisit join order; reduce the big-big join footprint by pre-aggregating or deduplicating first. • Tune shuffle partitions and executor memory only after reducing the data volume per task. • Persist selectively and unpersist aggressively if caching is needed for reuse.
productionExplanation: Frequent executor loss during a wide stage usually points to memory pressure, oversized partitions, or an execution plan that materializes too much data per task. Trade-offs: • More partitions reduce per-task memory but can increase shuffle overhead. • More memory may mask the problem without fixing an inefficient plan. • Pre-aggregation can lower cost dramatically, but you must ensure it preserves required detail. Monitoring and testing: • Capture executor lost count, task retry count, spill metrics, peak memory, and stage-level input/output row counts. • Benchmark representative peak-day data, not just average-day data. • Add regression tests on row explosion paths such as explode or one-to-many joins.
commonMistakes: Shuffle partitions are too large because the cluster or input size changed but partition counts did not.
The join order is poor, causing large intermediate datasets.
Exploding arrays or nested columns before filtering multiplies row counts.
Caching or previous stages already consumed executor memory.
followUps: What is the difference between spill and OOM in symptoms?
When would you change cluster size vs query plan?
How would you detect row explosion?

daily_totals.saveAsTextFile(output_path)

Stage progress: most tasks finished, one or more tasks are long-running.
Metrics to inspect: shuffle read, spill, skew ratio, executor lost count, file count.

transformations. Runtime doubled and shuffle traffic exploded.
hints: Whether you understand why some APIs are semantically correct but operationally expensive.

modelSolution: Fix the PySpark pipeline by addressing the root production issue, not just adding resources. • Switch to aggregateByKey, reduceByKey, or DataFrame aggregations that allow partial aggregation. • Combine related aggregates into one groupBy call when possible. • Project only the keys and measures required before the shuffle. • Review the business logic to see whether all rollup levels must be computed in one pipeline or can be materialized hierarchically.

Trade-offs: • Combined aggregations can make code denser and less readable. • Pre-aggregation reduces shuffle but may restrict late-stage flexibility. • Materializing intermediate rollups improves performance but adds storage and lineage complexity. Monitoring and testing: • Compare shuffle write/read, spill, and stage runtime before and after the change. • Add performance baselines for representative row counts so regressions are caught in CI or release checks.

title: The Small Files Avalanche
tags: Spark write path creates too many tiny files
businessContext: A bronze-to-silver pipeline writes partitioned Parquet to a data lake every 15 minutes.
problemStatement: The job technically succeeds, but downstream reads get slower over time and cloud object store listings become painfully expensive. Each partition contains hundreds or thousands of tiny files.
requirement: Fix the PySpark code so the pipeline is correct, scalable, and safe to rerun.
schema: DataFrames depend on the scenario. Assume large production-scale inputs, skewed keys, retries, and partitioned lake storage.
sampleInput: Use the code comments and logs to infer the input shape. Focus on the production failure mode, not local toy execution.
brokenCode:
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

hourly_df = spark.read.parquet(hourly_input_path)

# Broken: too many partitions and append-only hourly writes create thousands of tiny files.
(hourly_df
  .repartition(2000)
  .write
  .mode('append')
  .partitionBy('event_date', 'event_hour')
  .parquet(silver_path))
expectedOutput: Corrected PySpark code or approach should reduce the failure mode, preserve correctness, and include validation/monitoring.
logs:
[Spark] The Small Files Avalanche
Stage progress: most tasks finished, one or more tasks are long-running.
Metrics to inspect: shuffle read, spill, skew ratio, executor lost count, file count.
The job technically succeeds, but downstream reads get slower over time and cloud object store
listings become painfully expensive. Each partition contains hundreds or thousands of tiny files.
hints: Whether you understand storage layout as a performance problem, not just a write problem.
Writing with a very high number of output partitions.
Choose a partition scheme based on query patterns, not on every available column.
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix the broken PySpark code sample.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: Fix the PySpark pipeline by addressing the root production issue, not just adding resources. • Choose a partition scheme based on query patterns, not on every available column. • Control output file count using coalesce or repartition before write, but only after understanding the target partition cardinality. • Introduce periodic compaction or optimize jobs to merge small files into right-sized files. • Use time buckets such as date or hour instead of highly granular timestamps when query requirements allow it.
productionExplanation: Small files hurt metadata operations, open/close cost, compaction effort, and downstream planning time. This is often created by over-partitioning or too much task parallelism during writes. Trade-offs: • Coalescing can create larger files but may reduce parallelism for the write stage. • Compaction adds extra jobs and cost, but usually pays back through much faster reads. • Too coarse a partition strategy can improve file size but worsen partition pruning. Monitoring and testing: • Track average file size, files per partition, object listing latency, and scan planning time. • Add acceptance thresholds such as 'median file size above X MB' for production pipelines.
commonMistakes: Writing with a very high number of output partitions.
Partitioning by a high-cardinality column such as user_id or exact timestamp.
Micro-batches arriving too frequently with low data volume.
Using append mode without compaction strategy.
followUps: How do you pick an ideal file size?
Why not always use coalesce(1)?
What is the impact on downstream engines like Spark or Trino?

modelSolution: Fix the PySpark pipeline by addressing the root production issue, not just adding resources. • Audit each UDF and replace what you can with built-in functions such as regexp_replace, when, coalesce, and date functions. • If vectorization is unavoidable, evaluate pandas UDFs carefully with memory testing. • Move lookup logic to joins or reference tables rather than embedding it inside UDF code. • Keep UDF boundaries narrow and only after projection/filtering.

title: The Broadcast Betrayal
tags: Wrong broadcast choice causes timeout or memory issues
businessContext: An enrichment job joins a large fact table with a reference dataset that is usually small.
problemStatement: Someone forced a broadcast hint. It worked in test and smaller markets, but production now fails intermittently with broadcast timeout or executor memory pressure.
requirement: Fix the PySpark code so the pipeline is correct, scalable, and safe to rerun.
schema: DataFrames depend on the scenario. Assume large production-scale inputs, skewed keys, retries, and partitioned lake storage.
sampleInput: Use the code comments and logs to infer the input shape. Focus on the production failure mode, not local toy execution.
brokenCode:
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

events = spark.read.parquet(events_path)
customers = spark.read.parquet(customers_path)

# Broken: forced broadcast can crash executors if customers is no longer small.
enriched = events.join(broadcast(customers), on='customer_id', how='left')
enriched.write.mode('overwrite').parquet(output_path)
expectedOutput: Corrected PySpark code or approach should reduce the failure mode, preserve correctness, and include validation/monitoring.
logs:
[Spark] The Broadcast Betrayal
Stage progress: most tasks finished, one or more tasks are long-running.
Metrics to inspect: shuffle read, spill, skew ratio, executor lost count, file count.
Someone forced a broadcast hint. It worked in test and smaller markets, but production now fails
intermittently with broadcast timeout or executor memory pressure.
hints: Whether you know that broadcast is a strategy, not a magical optimization.

tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix the broken PySpark code sample.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: Fix the PySpark pipeline by addressing the root production issue, not just adding resources. • Remove the forced hint until the actual size and stability of the dimension are validated. • Estimate in-memory footprint, not just file size, and test with production-scale cardinality. • Consider bucketing, partitioned joins, or pre-filtering the dimension by relevant keys before joining. • Use adaptive query execution where appropriate, but still validate plan choices.
productionExplanation: Broadcast helps when one side is truly small, stable, and replicated safely to executors. A dataset that is 'smallish' in dev can be unsafe in prod. Trade-offs: • Broadcast avoids shuffle on one side but increases memory pressure on every executor. • Partitioned joins cost more network shuffle but are safer for growing dimensions. • Adaptive decisions are convenient, but they should not replace capacity awareness. Monitoring and testing: • Track broadcast size, timeout events, executor memory, and plan changes across releases. • Alert when reference-table volume crosses thresholds that invalidate broadcast assumptions.
commonMistakes: Reference data grew over time and no longer fits comfortably in executor memory.
Compressed size was mistaken for in-memory size.
Multiple concurrent broadcasts exist in the same query or cluster.
followUps: How would you estimate safe broadcast size?

Why might test pass but prod fail?

title: The Cache Everything Trap

filtered = raw.filter("event_date = '${run_date}'").cache()
joined = filtered.join(dim_customers, 'customer_id', 'left').cache()
result = joined.groupBy('customer_id').count().cache()
result.write.mode('overwrite').parquet(output_path)
expectedOutput: Corrected PySpark code or approach should reduce the failure mode, preserve correctness, and include validation/monitoring.

[Spark] The Cache Everything Trap
Stage progress: most tasks finished, one or more tasks are long-running.
Metrics to inspect: shuffle read, spill, skew ratio, executor lost count, file count.

caching was added.
hints: Whether you know that persistence is useful only when reuse outweighs its cost.

tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix the broken PySpark code sample.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: Fix the PySpark pipeline by addressing the root production issue, not just adding resources. • Map lineage and identify which datasets are actually reused multiple times. • Persist only the expensive and reused intermediates, and choose storage levels deliberately. • Unpersist as soon as downstream consumers are done. • Prefer materializing stable intermediate outputs when reuse spans jobs or days.
productionExplanation: Caching consumes memory and may trigger eviction, spill, or recomputation if applied indiscriminately. It is a tactical tool, not a default setting. Trade-offs: • Caching can reduce recomputation but increases memory pressure. • Disk-based persistence is safer than memory-only for large datasets but slower. • Materialized tables improve stability but add I/O and storage cost. Monitoring and testing: • Watch storage tab utilization, eviction count, spill metrics, and stage recomputation patterns. • Add pipeline profiling before and after persistence changes.
commonMistakes: Large DataFrames are cached even though they are used only once.
Cached datasets use MEMORY_ONLY and do not fit, causing recomputation and churn.
Intermediate DataFrames are cached before expensive filters or projections.
followUps: How do you choose a storage level?

title: The Backfill Explosion

businessContext: A pipeline that normally processes one day of data is asked to backfill six months for a regulatory request.
problemStatement: The same code that works daily now runs for many hours, overloads the cluster, and causes repeated failures.

    df = spark.read.parquet(raw_path)
    daily = df.filter(F.col('event_date') <= run_date)
    daily.write.mode('append').partitionBy('event_date').parquet(output_path)

repeated failures.
hints: Whether you can reason about scale-up risk and redesign for historical replay.

modelSolution: Fix the PySpark pipeline by addressing the root production issue, not just adding resources. • Split the backfill into bounded windows such as day or week and orchestrate them independently. • Separate compute pools for backfills when possible so SLA-sensitive jobs are insulated. • Materialize reusable reference datasets once and reuse them across windows. • Validate idempotent writes and partition-level reruns before launching the full backfill.
productionExplanation: Code that is stable for a one-day slice may not be stable for a multi-month backfill because data volume, skew patterns, and output layout change drastically. Trade-offs: • Smaller windows improve recoverability but add orchestration overhead. • Dedicated backfill clusters improve safety but increase cost. • Parallel window execution accelerates completion but can saturate storage or metastore services. Monitoring and testing: • Track per-window runtime, failure rate, output partition count, and rerun safety. • Use checkpoints or manifest tables so partially completed windows are visible and resumable.

Trade-offs: • Strong exactly-once-like behavior usually requires more design effort at the sink. • Resetting checkpoints can restore liveness quickly but may require full replay and duplicate protection. • Longer watermarks improve correctness for late events but enlarge state. Monitoring and testing: • Monitor consumer lag, state-store size, late-event counts, duplicate rate at the sink, and checkpoint health. • Exercise failure drills for restart, rollback, and state migration.

modelSolution: Fix the PySpark pipeline by addressing the root production issue, not just adding resources. • Inspect the scan plan and verify which partitions were actually read rather than trusting the SQL text. • Rewrite predicates to target the physical partition column directly and as early as possible. • Check whether the view or upstream transformation is preventing pushdown or pruning. • Document the difference between business-time logic and storage partition logic so analysts and engineers use the right filter shape.
productionExplanation: The problem is that a filter can be present in code without being useful for pruning. If the expression wraps the partition column, uses the wrong timezone, or applies the predicate after a view transformation, Spark may still touch far more data than expected. Trade-offs: • Physical partitioning improves scan efficiency, but only when queries are written to exploit it. • Abstracted views are convenient, but they can hide critical storage details if teams never inspect plans. • Designing around storage dates may be operationally efficient, though it requires care when business reporting uses local time. Monitoring and testing: • Track scan bytes, partitions touched, filter selectivity, and plan operators showing whether pruning occurred. • Add query examples or linting for common partitioned datasets so teams do not wrap partition columns in non-prunable expressions. • Compare estimated versus actual partitions scanned after schema or view changes.

title: The History Table Dilemma

tasks: Diagnose the issue.

modelSolution: • Use a surrogate key per version and store business key, effective_start_date, effective_end_date, and current_flag. • When a change arrives, expire the old current record and insert a new version. • Join facts to the correct dimension version using the fact event date and the validity range.

title: The Merge Contention Problem
tags: Concurrent upserts create lock contention or deadlocks
businessContext: An operational warehouse receives frequent micro-batch merges into a customer status table while analysts and downstream loads query the same objects.
problemStatement: During peak hours, merges slow down, block each other, or deadlock.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Concurrent upserts create lock contention or deadlocks
brokenCode:
SELECT
  customer_id,
  order_status,
  SUM(amount) AS revenue
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id, order_status;
logs: During peak hours, merges slow down, block each other, or deadlock.
hints: Whether you can discuss concurrency, transaction scope, and workload isolation.
Multiple jobs update the same key ranges at the same time.
Reduce the scope of each merge by partition pruning or key-range isolation.
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Reduce the scope of each merge by partition pruning or key-range isolation. • Pre-stage the incoming changes so the final merge is narrow and deterministic. • Serialize conflicting writers for the same domain if true parallelism is unsafe. • Consider append-plus-compaction patterns if the platform handles them better than row-level contention.
productionExplanation: Upsert logic is not only a SQL problem; it is a concurrency design problem. Large merge scopes and overlapping key ranges can create lock contention. Trade-offs: • Serialization reduces deadlocks but also limits throughput. • Micro-batches improve latency but can worsen contention if they overlap heavily. • Append-and-compact improves write concurrency but complicates current-state reads. Monitoring and testing: • Monitor lock wait time, deadlock frequency, keys touched per merge, and transaction duration. • Test concurrency with overlapping key patterns, not just single-job functional tests.
commonMistakes: Multiple jobs update the same key ranges at the same time.
Each merge scans too much of the target because predicates are broad.
Transactions stay open too long while computing match logic.
followUps: How would you split key ranges safely?

modelSolution: • Prefer CDC or database log-based extraction for consistency and lower source impact. • If snapshot reads are required, use database capabilities that guarantee a point-in-time consistent view. • Tag extracts with source high-water marks so downstream reconciliation understands the exact capture point.

title: The Top-N Tie Trap
tags: Window-function ranking returns unstable or duplicated Top-N results
businessContext: A product team asks for the top 3 products by revenue in each category. The query uses a window function and seems correct on first inspection.
problemStatement: Different runs or engine migrations produce slightly different results whenever ties occur, and stakeholders question why the dashboard is not stable.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Window-function ranking returns unstable or duplicated Top-N results
brokenCode:
SELECT
  o.order_id,
  SUM(p.payment_amount) - COALESCE(SUM(r.refund_amount), 0) AS net_revenue
FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
LEFT JOIN refunds r ON o.order_id = r.order_id
GROUP BY o.order_id;

stakeholders question why the dashboard is not stable.
hints: Whether you understand that ranking logic needs deterministic tie-breaking, not just the right window function name.
The query uses row_number, rank, or dense_rank without understanding the tie behavior.

tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Clarify the business rule first: do stakeholders want exactly N rows, or all tied rows at the cutoff? • Add deterministic secondary ordering using a stable business key when exact ordering matters. • Choose the ranking function that matches the requirement and document the tie behavior in the semantic layer. • Validate the result on known tie cases instead of only on unique-metric examples.
productionExplanation: The issue is not the idea of ranking itself. It is that 'top 3' is ambiguous when multiple rows share the same metric unless you define deterministic ordering and business rules for ties. Trade-offs: • Returning all ties is fairer analytically, but it may violate UX expectations for an exact-sized leaderboard. • Returning exactly N rows is operationally simpler, but only if the tie-breaker is defensible and consistent. • Adding deterministic ordering improves stability, though it can create business debates about what the secondary sort should be. Monitoring and testing: • Track tie frequency on the ranked metric, result-set size by category, and variance between runs on the same input. • Add fixtures where several rows share the cutoff metric. • Document the chosen tie rule in dashboards and data contracts so downstream teams do not infer a different definition.

title: The FX Conversion Mismatch
tags: Currency conversion logic creates inconsistent financial reporting
businessContext: A global revenue mart converts transactions from local currencies into a reporting currency for dashboards and month-end summaries.
problemStatement: Different teams get different totals because some queries use transaction-date rates, others use month-end rates, and refunds are handled inconsistently.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Currency conversion logic creates inconsistent financial reporting
brokenCode:
SELECT
  customer_id,
  order_status,
  SUM(amount) AS revenue
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id, order_status;
logs:
Different teams get different totals because some queries use transaction-date rates, others use
month-end rates, and refunds are handled inconsistently.
hints: Whether you can identify grain, business definitions, and temporal logic issues in financial SQL design.
Different metrics are using different effective-date rules for FX rates.
Define conversion policy by metric type - for example booked revenue, recognized revenue, and
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Define conversion policy by metric type - for example booked revenue, recognized revenue, and accounting restatement may each use different effective dates. • Join FX rates at the correct grain and retain both original currency amounts and converted amounts with the applied rate metadata. • Standardize refund and reversal logic so it is clear whether they use original transaction rates, event-date rates, or finance restatement rules. • Expose governed metric tables or semantic models so analysts do not reinvent FX logic in every report.
productionExplanation: This is a modeling and definition problem. Currency conversion is only reliable when the warehouse is explicit about which rate table, date, and restatement policy each metric uses. Trade-offs: • Centralizing FX conversion improves consistency, but it requires tight business agreement and change management. • Keeping both original and converted values adds storage, but it dramatically improves explainability. • Multiple valid conversion views may exist, but they must be intentionally separated rather than accidentally mixed. Monitoring and testing: • Track rate coverage, fallback-rate usage, differences between booked and restated metrics, and reconciliation against finance controls. • Add tests around month boundaries, rate gaps, and refund scenarios. • Version the conversion policy so historical reports can be explained after logic changes.
commonMistakes: Different metrics are using different effective-date rules for FX rates.
Rate tables are joined at the wrong grain or with missing fallback behavior.
Refunds and reversals are converted using current rates instead of the original booked logic required
The semantic layer never encoded the business definition, so analysts improvised in ad hoc SQL.
followUps: Which date would you use for FX conversion?
How do you handle missing rates?
Why keep both local and converted amounts?

title: The JSON Fanout Query
tags: Flattening semi-structured fields in SQL creates double counting and high cost
businessContext: A warehouse table stores order-level rows with a semi-structured JSON payload for promotions, discounts, and fulfillment events.
problemStatement: A new query flattens multiple arrays out of the JSON and joins them back to orders, but revenue and counts become inflated while runtime increases sharply.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Flattening semi-structured fields in SQL creates double counting and high cost
brokenCode:
SELECT
  customer_id,
  order_status,
  SUM(amount) AS revenue
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id, order_status;
logs:
A new query flattens multiple arrays out of the JSON and joins them back to orders, but revenue
and counts become inflated while runtime increases sharply.
hints: Whether you can reason about table grain when semi-structured data is flattened inside SQL.
Multiple arrays were unnested in the same query without controlling the resulting grain.
State the grain before and after each flatten step, and decide whether the analysis should remain at
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • State the grain before and after each flatten step, and decide whether the analysis should remain at order grain or move to a child-event grain. • Pre-aggregate child collections back to the required grain before joining them to the parent order facts. • Where the pattern is common, build curated child tables or normalized views so analysts do not flatten raw JSON differently every time. • Validate revenue and row counts against control totals after each grain-changing operation.
productionExplanation: The problem is not JSON itself. The issue is that flattening nested arrays changes the grain, and once several child collections are expanded together the query can accidentally multiply the parent facts. Trade-offs: • Flattening in SQL is flexible for exploration, but expensive and easy to misuse repeatedly. • Curated child tables improve correctness and performance, though they add pipeline maintenance. • Keeping raw semi-structured payloads preserves fidelity, but downstream models still need explicit grain management. Monitoring and testing: • Track row counts before and after unnesting, revenue control totals, and the cost of repeated JSON flatten operations. • Create unit tests for representative payloads with multiple child arrays. • Document the curated grain of any helper views built on top of the raw payload.
commonMistakes: Multiple arrays were unnested in the same query without controlling the resulting grain.
The analyst expected order-level totals while operating on a child-event grain.
The warehouse lacks curated child tables, so raw flattening is happening repeatedly in ad hoc SQL.
Discount and promotion logic is being applied after the grain has already exploded.
followUps: How can multiple unnests multiply rows?
When would you build child tables instead of flattening on demand?
Why is DISTINCT not a reliable fix here?

title: The MERGE with Duplicate Matches
tags: MERGE fails or behaves unpredictably because source keys are not unique
businessContext: A warehouse pipeline uses MERGE to upsert customer status into a curated table based on customer_id.
problemStatement: Some loads fail with multiple-match errors, while others succeed but produce hard-to-explain outcomes because the source contains duplicate keys in the same batch.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: MERGE fails or behaves unpredictably because source keys are not unique
brokenCode:
SELECT
  o.order_id,
  SUM(p.payment_amount) - COALESCE(SUM(r.refund_amount), 0) AS net_revenue
FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
LEFT JOIN refunds r ON o.order_id = r.order_id
GROUP BY o.order_id;
logs:
Some loads fail with multiple-match errors, while others succeed but produce hard-to-explain
outcomes because the source contains duplicate keys in the same batch.
hints: Whether you understand that upsert logic depends on source uniqueness and deterministic match rules.
The source delta contains retries, duplicates, or multiple state changes for the same key in one load
Pre-stage the source batch and reduce it to one deterministic record per business key before MERGE
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Pre-stage the source batch and reduce it to one deterministic record per business key before MERGE - often using event time, ingestion time, and source priority rules. • Validate uniqueness before the final upsert and fail fast if the batch violates the contract. • If the business needs to preserve multiple changes from one load window, model change history separately and derive current state from that controlled history. • Keep the final MERGE narrow and deterministic so the warehouse engine is not asked to resolve ambiguity.
productionExplanation: The target MERGE statement may be syntactically correct, but if the incoming source batch contains more than one candidate row per business key, the behavior becomes ambiguous or engine-specific. Trade-offs: • Strict uniqueness validation improves trust, but it may stop the pipeline more often until upstream quality improves. • Auto-deduplicating keeps the flow moving, but only if the tie-break rule is explicit and defensible. • Storing history plus current state is more robust, though it adds extra modeling and storage. Monitoring and testing: • Track duplicate-source-key rate, MERGE failure frequency, and the share of batches requiring deterministic tie-break logic. • Test cases where the same key appears multiple times with different event timestamps and statuses. • Alert when the source batch violates the one-row-per-key assumption before reaching the final target table.
commonMistakes: The source delta contains retries, duplicates, or multiple state changes for the same key in one load
The pipeline never deduplicated or ordered source changes before MERGE.
The target match condition is broader than intended, allowing multiple target or source matches.
The team assumed MERGE would magically resolve contradictory source rows.
followUps: How would you choose the winning record?
Would you ever let MERGE handle duplicates directly?
When should you keep full change history instead?

title: The Timezone Boundary Bug
tags: Date-based reports are wrong because local and UTC boundaries are mixed
businessContext: A global product dashboard reports daily signups and purchases by market. The warehouse stores event timestamps in UTC, but the business reviews performance by local market date.
problemStatement: Counts near midnight look wrong in several countries, and month-end totals differ between dashboards built by different teams.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Date-based reports are wrong because local and UTC boundaries are mixed
brokenCode:
SELECT
  customer_id,
  order_status,
  SUM(amount) AS revenue
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id, order_status;
logs:
Counts near midnight look wrong in several countries, and month-end totals differ between
dashboards built by different teams.
hints: Whether you understand that time-based SQL logic requires an explicit business-time definition, not just date_trunc.
One query groups by UTC date while another converts to local market time first.
Define the reporting-time standard explicitly for each subject area and market.
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Define the reporting-time standard explicitly for each subject area and market. • Create governed derived date columns or calendar dimensions for the required business timezone instead of letting every analyst convert timestamps independently. • Keep raw UTC timestamps for operational traceability, but build local-date derivations consistently in curated models. • Test edge cases around midnight, daylight-saving transitions, and month-end boundaries.
productionExplanation: The issue is that UTC storage is correct operationally, but business reporting often needs local calendar boundaries. If teams derive dates inconsistently, totals shift around midnight and at period boundaries. Trade-offs: • UTC storage keeps ingestion and cross-system comparison simple, but it is not automatically the right reporting date. • Creating multiple governed time perspectives improves correctness, though it increases modeling discipline and documentation needs. • Letting analysts convert time ad hoc feels flexible, but it usually creates silent divergence. Monitoring and testing: • Track differences between UTC-date and local-date aggregates for key markets, especially near boundary hours. • Add tests for daylight-saving changes and end-of-month periods. • Document every date field clearly so consumers know whether it is UTC, local, booking, or processing date.

title: The Snapshot Join Trap
tags: Joining periodic snapshots to transactions creates misleading metrics
businessContext: A retail warehouse stores daily inventory snapshots and separate sales transactions. An analyst joins them directly to explain stock coverage and sell-through.
problemStatement: The resulting query runs, but inventory metrics are overstated or misaligned because the snapshot grain and transaction grain do not line up cleanly.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Joining periodic snapshots to transactions creates misleading metrics
brokenCode:
SELECT
  o.order_id,
  SUM(p.payment_amount) - COALESCE(SUM(r.refund_amount), 0) AS net_revenue
FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
LEFT JOIN refunds r ON o.order_id = r.order_id
GROUP BY o.order_id;
logs:
The resulting query runs, but inventory metrics are overstated or misaligned because the snapshot
grain and transaction grain do not line up cleanly.
hints: Whether you understand the difference between event facts and periodic snapshot facts.
The query joins a state table and an event table on product only, ignoring snapshot date logic.
Define the question first: stock on hand at close, average stock, sell-through over a window, or
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Define the question first: stock on hand at close, average stock, sell-through over a window, or stockout exposure all need different logic. • Aggregate transactions to the same reporting period before combining them with snapshots, or use point-in-time joins with explicit temporal conditions. • Preserve the snapshot as a state table and avoid treating it like another transactional fact. • Document the semantic difference in the model so analysts know that product-level joins are not enough.
productionExplanation: This is a grain-and-time problem. Inventory snapshots represent state at a point in time, while sales transactions represent events. Joining them directly without a clear temporal rule usually creates nonsense. Trade-offs: • Snapshot tables make state analysis possible, but they require more careful temporal joins than event facts. • Aggregating transactions before joining reduces misuse, though it can limit very detailed drill-down in the same query. • Keeping separate semantic models improves clarity, but requires more governed datasets. Monitoring and testing: • Track reconciliation between snapshot balances and transaction-based inventory movements, and watch for inflated counts after joins. • Add tests for point-in-time logic and period-based sell-through calculations. • Document whether the snapshot is start-of-day, end-of-day, or another timing convention.
commonMistakes: The query joins a state table and an event table on product only, ignoring snapshot date logic.
Multiple transaction rows attach to the same snapshot row and multiply inventory balances.
The analysis mixes beginning-of-day, end-of-day, and intra-day semantics without stating which one
The warehouse model does not document the grain and meaning of the snapshot table clearly.
followUps: What is the difference between a periodic snapshot and a transaction fact?
How would you calculate sell-through safely?
Why does a simple join on product inflate metrics?

title: The Chasm Trap
tags: Joining multiple fact tables through shared dimensions produces fanout
businessContext: A BI team wants one wide query combining ad impressions, website sessions, and orders by campaign and day.
problemStatement: They join three fact tables through common dimensions and produce impressive-looking dashboards, but conversion and revenue numbers are inflated in subtle ways.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Joining multiple fact tables through shared dimensions produces fanout
brokenCode:
SELECT
  o.order_id,
  SUM(p.payment_amount) - COALESCE(SUM(r.refund_amount), 0) AS net_revenue
FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
LEFT JOIN refunds r ON o.order_id = r.order_id
GROUP BY o.order_id;
logs:
They join three fact tables through common dimensions and produce impressive-looking
dashboards, but conversion and revenue numbers are inflated in subtle ways.
hints: Whether you can recognize the classic chasm-trap problem in warehouse querying and semantic design.
The team joined impression, session, and order facts directly instead of aggregating them separately.
Aggregate each fact table to the reporting grain independently - for example campaign and day -
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Aggregate each fact table to the reporting grain independently - for example campaign and day - before combining the metrics. • Use conformed dimensions for alignment, but do not use them as a license to directly join raw fact tables together. • Where possible, expose governed metric marts or semantic models that already combine safe aggregated measures. • Clarify attribution and grain rules so analysts know which metrics can truly be compared side by side.
productionExplanation: The issue is that multiple fact tables are each valid at their own grain, but when you join them naively through shared dimensions, the rows multiply across unrelated facts. Trade-offs: • Separate aggregation is more correct and often cheaper, but it limits raw cross-fact drill-through in one query. • Wide semantic marts improve usability, though they add maintenance and governance responsibilities. • Letting analysts join facts freely seems flexible, but it creates silent mathematical errors. Monitoring and testing: • Track row-count inflation in multi-fact queries, variance against control totals, and the most common fact-to-fact join patterns in BI usage. • Create certification checks for governed dashboards that mix multiple fact domains. • Document the grain of every fact and the safe paths for combining them.
commonMistakes: The team joined impression, session, and order facts directly instead of aggregating them separately.
The shared dimensions were treated as if they created one clean common grain, but they do not.
The semantic layer does not protect analysts from many-to-many fanout across facts.
Different facts use slightly different attribution rules, making naive joins even more misleading.
followUps: What is a chasm trap?
Why don't shared dimensions solve it?
When would you build a semantic mart for this use case?

title: The Distinct Count at Scale
tags: High-cardinality distinct counting becomes expensive and inconsistent
businessContext: A stakeholder wants daily active users, weekly active users, and cross-channel unique customers across a large event estate.
problemStatement: Different teams compute the metrics with different SQL patterns, runtimes are high, and slight logic differences create disagreement about the official number.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: High-cardinality distinct counting becomes expensive and inconsistent
brokenCode:
SELECT
  customer_id,
  order_status,
  SUM(amount) AS revenue
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id, order_status;
logs:
Different teams compute the metrics with different SQL patterns, runtimes are high, and slight logic
differences create disagreement about the official number.
hints: Whether you can discuss correctness, scale, and semantic consistency for distinct-count metrics.
Each team defines the identity key or activity rule slightly differently.
First define the metric contract clearly - identity key, activity event types, lookback window, and
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • First define the metric contract clearly - identity key, activity event types, lookback window, and late-data policy. • For common heavy-use metrics, build curated intermediate tables such as daily user activity sets or governed aggregates instead of repeatedly scanning raw events. • Decide where exactness is mandatory and where approximate techniques are acceptable for exploratory or high-scale monitoring use cases. • Keep the semantic definition centralized so weekly and monthly uniques are derived consistently from the same identity logic.
productionExplanation: Distinct counting is deceptively simple. At scale it becomes a question of business definition, exact versus approximate trade-offs, and whether the warehouse should repeatedly recompute the same expensive uniqueness logic. Trade-offs: • Exact distinct counts maximize confidence, but they are often expensive at large scale. • Approximate techniques improve speed and cost, but only when business users accept the error bounds. • Precomputed activity sets improve consistency, though they add data-maintenance logic and freshness considerations. Monitoring and testing: • Track query cost for distinct metrics, divergence between teams' definitions, and late-data impact on previously published counts. • Test identity-resolution changes and ensure they restate or annotate downstream active-user series correctly. • Version metric definitions so historical dashboard changes can be explained.
commonMistakes: Each team defines the identity key or activity rule slightly differently.
Exact distinct counts over very large raw tables are repeatedly recomputed in dashboards.
Late events and identity-resolution changes alter the apparent unique population after the fact.
No governed serving layer exists for common active-user metrics.
followUps: When would approximate distinct counts be acceptable?
How would you precompute active-user metrics safely?
Why do distinct metrics often drift across teams?

title: The Restatement vs Close Debate
tags: Closed financial periods conflict with late-arriving changes
businessContext: A finance data mart publishes month-end revenue and margin numbers. A few days later, late invoices, chargebacks, and source corrections still arrive for the same period.
problemStatement: Business teams disagree on whether historical dashboards should change, whether the closed month should stay frozen, and how to reconcile operational truth with published finance truth.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Closed financial periods conflict with late-arriving changes
brokenCode:
SELECT
  customer_id,
  order_status,
  SUM(amount) AS revenue
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id, order_status;
logs:
Business teams disagree on whether historical dashboards should change, whether the closed
month should stay frozen, and how to reconcile operational truth with published finance truth.
hints: Whether you can navigate the tension between late data and period-close governance.
The same tables are serving both live operational analytics and closed finance reporting.
Model closed-period reporting explicitly - for example with period snapshots, restatement flags, or
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Model closed-period reporting explicitly - for example with period snapshots, restatement flags, or adjustment tables that preserve what was published when. • Separate operational current-state views from finance-certified close outputs so one does not silently rewrite the other. • Document whether late records restate the original period, appear as current-period adjustments, or are shown through both lenses for different audiences. • Persist close metadata such as freeze time, approval version, and adjustment reason.
productionExplanation: This is not just a late-arriving-data problem. It is about whether the warehouse supports both operational current truth and officially closed reporting snapshots. Trade-offs: • Frozen closes improve auditability and stakeholder trust, but they are not the same as current operational truth. • Fully restated historical dashboards are analytically clean, but they can conflict with what finance officially signed off on. • Supporting both perspectives improves clarity, though it adds modeling and governance complexity. Monitoring and testing: • Track late-arrival volume after period close, differences between closed and current views, and adjustment volume by reason. • Reconcile finance-certified outputs to operational restated views regularly so differences remain explainable. • Test month-end logic with realistic late-invoice and chargeback scenarios.

There is no concept of period-close snapshot, freeze, or restatement policy in the data model.

hints: Whether you understand that orchestration success is not the same as data success.

tasks: Diagnose the issue.

modelSolution: • Separate technical success from data quality success by adding validation tasks and explicit fail conditions. • Check file completeness, row counts, schema validity, and business thresholds before publishing. • Use a staging-to-publish pattern so bad data never becomes the official table.

title: The Retry Illusion
tags: Automatic retries hide a deterministic data bug
businessContext: A DAG is configured with generous retries because leadership wants fewer failed runs.
problemStatement: One transform task intermittently succeeds on the third or fourth retry, but the resulting data is inconsistent and the root cause remains unresolved.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Automatic retries hide a deterministic data bug

[Airflow] The Retry Illusion

Inspect: try_number, input rows, output rows, partition freshness, and idempotency.

hints: Whether you can explain why retries are for transient failures, not logic failures.
The task fails because of malformed input or code bugs, not temporary infrastructure issues.
Make tasks fail fast on deterministic validation issues and do not rely on retries to 'heal' them.
tasks: Diagnose the issue.

Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Make tasks fail fast on deterministic validation issues and do not rely on retries to 'heal' them. • Differentiate retryable exceptions from non-retryable ones. • Ensure task actions are idempotent so a legitimate retry does not duplicate data.

title: The Dynamic Overwrite Blast Radius
tags: Partition overwrite logic replaces more data than intended
businessContext: A daily batch pipeline rewrites only the partitions it processed, using dynamic partition overwrite behavior against a curated table.
problemStatement: After a code or schema change, the job deletes or replaces a wider slice than intended, and downstream teams discover that previously healthy partitions have disappeared.

modelSolution: • Treat overwrite scope as a first-class contract and validate the exact partition set before commit. • Stage the output and compare expected versus actual partition values prior to promotion. • Protect high-value tables with guardrails that reject abnormally broad overwrite attempts. • Keep recovery options such as versioned tables, rollback, or restore playbooks ready for partition-write incidents.

Trade-offs: • Partition overwrite is simpler than row-level merge for batch replacement, but its blast radius can be severe if scope drifts. • Strict partition validation improves safety, though it adds some pre-commit overhead. • Transactional table formats help recovery, but they do not replace the need for scope controls. Monitoring and testing: • Track partitions written per run, abnormal partition-value changes, overwrite-versus-expected partition counts, and restore incidents. • Alert when a run attempts to touch far more partitions than the normal baseline. • Test schema and partition evolution changes in a staging environment with realistic write patterns.

title: The Rewrite Amplification Problem
tags: Small logical updates cause large physical file rewrites
businessContext: A lakehouse table supports updates for current-state records, and business stakeholders assume small daily corrections should be cheap because only a few rows changed.
problemStatement: In practice, the update workflow rewrites many files, consumes large compute budgets, and slows both writers and readers.

modelSolution: • Measure rewrite amplification explicitly - how many files and bytes are rewritten per logical change set. • Organize or cluster the table around the update keys or recency windows that are mutated most often. • Batch small corrections where business latency allows so the platform rewrites fewer times with better file locality. • For especially high-churn domains, reconsider whether the table should be modeled as append-plus-derived-current-state instead of frequent in-place updates.

Trade-offs: • Better clustering improves update efficiency, but may not perfectly match every read path. • Batching updates lowers rewrite overhead, though it can increase freshness lag. • Append-plus-derive patterns are often cheaper operationally, but they complicate current-state serving logic. Monitoring and testing: • Track rewrite amplification ratio, files rewritten per update run, read latency after update windows, and maintenance cost for high-churn tables. • Compare operational cost between frequent small updates and less frequent batched corrections. • Review whether update-heavy tables are still aligned with their intended workload design.

title: The Data Skipping Disappointment
tags: Table statistics exist, but queries still scan far more than expected
businessContext: A platform team promotes data skipping, clustering, or file-level statistics as a way to improve selective reads on large tables.
problemStatement: Teams then discover that some important queries still scan heavily, leading to disappointment and confusion about why the advertised optimization is not working well.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Table statistics exist, but queries still scan far more than expected

confusion about why the advertised optimization is not working well.
hints: Whether you understand when file-level pruning techniques help and when they do not.
The columns used in filters are not well clustered in the physical layout.
Inspect actual files scanned and compare that with the filtered value distribution to see whether the
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Inspect actual files scanned and compare that with the filtered value distribution to see whether the layout really supports skipping. • Use clustering, sorting, or partitioning deliberately on the columns that matter most for selective reads. • Avoid over-promising; document which predicate patterns benefit from skipping and which ones still need broader scans. • For heavily used workloads, test query patterns before and after layout changes rather than relying on generic optimization claims.
productionExplanation: The root issue is that skipping only helps when the data layout and statistics line up with the query predicates. If values are widely scattered, nested, or poorly ordered, file-level stats may offer limited selectivity. Trade-offs: • Skipping is cheaper than over-partitioning for some workloads, but it is not a substitute for all layout design. • Strong clustering improves selective reads, though maintenance cost rises as data churn increases. • Supporting many different filter patterns equally well is hard; optimizing for the most important ones requires product decisions. Monitoring and testing: • Track files scanned versus total files, selectivity by predicate type, clustering effectiveness, and maintenance cost for the chosen layout strategy. • Benchmark representative queries after each layout change. • Review whether business-critical filter patterns have changed over time and re-evaluate layout accordingly.
commonMistakes: The columns used in filters are not well clustered in the physical layout.
Queries filter on transformed or nested expressions that do not map cleanly to the stored stats.
Files contain a wide range of values, so min-max metadata is not very selective.
The team expected data skipping to compensate for fundamentally poor partitioning or file
organization.
followUps: What kinds of filters benefit most from data skipping?

title: The Clone vs Copy Decision
tags: Teams need safe experimentation without corrupting production tables
businessContext: An analytics team wants to test a major schema change and transformation rewrite on a large production-like table.
problemStatement: Some engineers propose copying the entire table to a new location, while others suggest cloning, snapshotting, or creating a shallow environment-specific branch depending on platform capabilities.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Teams need safe experimentation without corrupting production tables

snapshotting, or creating a shallow environment-specific branch depending on platform capabilities.
hints: Whether you can discuss safe experimentation patterns in a lakehouse and their trade-offs.
The team lacks clarity on whether they need independent mutation, just read-only comparison, or a
Clarify the experiment goal first: read-only validation, isolated write testing, rollback checkpoint, or
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Clarify the experiment goal first: read-only validation, isolated write testing, rollback checkpoint, or long-lived branch-like development. • Choose the lightest mechanism that still provides the required isolation and lifecycle - for example snapshot, clone, or full copy depending on platform behavior. • Protect production permissions carefully so the experiment cannot accidentally write back to the live dataset. • Document the promotion and cleanup plan before creating large experimental branches that linger indefinitely.
productionExplanation: The question is really about how to create a realistic but safe working environment without incurring unnecessary storage cost or risking writes against the production source of truth. Trade-offs: • Full copies maximize independence, but they are expensive in storage and time. • Lighter clone or snapshot approaches are faster and cheaper, though teams must understand what metadata or storage they still share. • Temporary sandboxes improve safety, but they can create governance sprawl if never cleaned up. Monitoring and testing: • Track clone or sandbox age, storage growth from experimental datasets, and number of incidents or near misses involving test environments. • Label experimental clones clearly and enforce lifecycle cleanup. • Review whether teams are using full copies where a cheaper isolated snapshot would have been enough.

hints: Whether you understand that schema evolution is not only about new columns - type changes can be far more dangerous.

Treat numeric type changes as high-risk schema evolution and review them explicitly rather than
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Treat numeric type changes as high-risk schema evolution and review them explicitly rather than auto-merging them casually. • Preserve raw fidelity in the bronze layer, but normalize or cast carefully in curated layers with documented business rules. • Test downstream aggregations, joins, and exports for overflow, rounding, and precision loss. • Version contracts with producer teams so consumers know whether the widened precision changes business meaning.
productionExplanation: This is a contract problem. Numeric type changes can break reads, widen storage, or silently change rounding and aggregation behavior if downstream layers are not prepared. Trade-offs: • Auto-merging type changes improves ingestion resilience, but it can silently push complexity onto downstream consumers. • Strict rejection protects curated layers, though it may delay ingestion during source changes. • Keeping raw and curated typing separate improves safety, but adds more transformation governance. Monitoring and testing: • Track schema-change incidents by type, failed casts, precision-loss warnings, and reconciliation variance after numeric evolution events. • Add tests for high-precision values and boundary conditions. • Review all downstream consumers of critical monetary fields whenever precision or scale changes.

state, or delayed visibility, creating cross-tool inconsistency.
hints: Whether you can reason about multi-engine interoperability and metadata synchronization.

Map the exact read path of each consumer - native transaction log, manifest, external catalog, or
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Map the exact read path of each consumer - native transaction log, manifest, external catalog, or cached metadata - and identify where freshness can diverge. • Refresh or regenerate external metadata deliberately as part of the publish contract when multi-engine support is required. • Set expectations on freshness and consistency per consumer path instead of promising identical visibility everywhere by default. • For critical datasets, prefer a narrower set of supported consumption patterns rather than an uncontrolled many-tool ecosystem.
productionExplanation: The table may be transactionally consistent in its native engine, but that does not guarantee every external consumer sees the latest state at the same time or through the same metadata path. Trade-offs: • Broad interoperability increases platform reach, but it also adds consistency management and operational complexity. • Strictly supporting only native readers simplifies correctness, though it may limit business adoption. • Manifest or cache refresh steps improve compatibility, but they introduce lag and more moving parts. Monitoring and testing: • Track freshness lag by consumer engine, metadata refresh failures, and incidents caused by stale external views. • Certify only the access paths that meet documented consistency expectations. • Review whether high-value datasets should expose fewer, more controlled consumption interfaces.

title: The Erasure Request Challenge
tags: Personal-data deletion requests collide with append-only historical design
businessContext: A platform receives regulatory or contractual requests to erase a user's personal data from analytics storage.
problemStatement: The data exists across raw, refined, and serving layers, and some datasets are intentionally append-only for audit or replay. Teams struggle to honor deletion without breaking lineage or creating hidden remnants.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Personal-data deletion requests collide with append-only historical design

remnants.
hints: Whether you can discuss deletion semantics, governance, and practical implementation across lakehouse layers.

tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Build or use a data inventory that maps sensitive fields and derived datasets touched by the subject identifier. • Define layer-specific treatment: physical deletion, token removal, anonymization, or retention exception where legally justified and documented. • Propagate deletion through derived tables, features, and serving layers using a controlled workflow rather than one-off manual edits. • Keep auditable records of the deletion request and completion status without retaining the very personal data you were asked to remove.
productionExplanation: This is both a governance and architecture problem. Personal data may exist in many physical and derived forms, and deleting it safely requires inventory, propagation rules, and clarity on what must be removed versus retained in anonymized form. Trade-offs: • Strict deletion improves compliance, but it can complicate replay, audit, and current-state derivations. • Anonymization can preserve some analytical value, though it may not satisfy every regulatory request if re-identification remains possible. • Central deletion workflows add platform complexity, but ad hoc manual deletion is far riskier. Monitoring and testing: • Track deletion request backlog, completion coverage by layer, residual sensitive-data findings, and time-to-propagate deletion across serving systems. • Run periodic scans for orphan identifiers left behind in derived datasets. • Test deletion workflows on synthetic end-to-end identities before applying them in production.

title: The CDC Bootstrap Merge

stream overlap imperfectly.
hints: Whether you understand the tricky handoff between snapshot bootstrap and live CDC ingestion.

Define the exact bootstrap boundary - typically a source sequence position or time window - and
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Define the exact bootstrap boundary - typically a source sequence position or time window - and persist it as metadata. • Load the snapshot as a baseline, then apply CDC changes deterministically based on source ordering after or across the chosen boundary. • Deduplicate by business key plus source sequence semantics, not just by arrival time. • Reconcile the resulting current state against the source after cutover before declaring the target trustworthy.

Without a clear boundary or sequence rule, the target can apply the same business change twice or in the wrong order. Trade-offs: • Starting CDC early reduces downtime risk, but it complicates ordering and deduplication. • A clean stop-the-world snapshot is simpler conceptually, though often impractical for busy systems. • Extra reconciliation steps improve confidence, but they add time to the cutover process. Monitoring and testing: • Track duplicate-key rate during bootstrap, lag between snapshot boundary and CDC catch-up, and reconciliation mismatches after cutover. • Retain metadata about the boundary position used for the initial load. • Test the bootstrap path using synthetic concurrent updates around the chosen cutover point.

title: The Orphan File Incident

businessContext: A team experiences a failed job during a write operation, and later storage scans reveal files under the table path that do not appear in the current transaction history.

title: The Consumer Lag Crisis
tags: Kafka consumers fall behind during traffic spikes
businessContext: A real-time analytics pipeline consumes user events from Kafka and writes curated aggregates every few minutes.
problemStatement: During campaigns and product launches, consumer lag spikes badly and downstream freshness SLAs are missed.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Kafka consumers fall behind during traffic spikes
logs:
[Kafka] The Consumer Lag Crisis
Consumer symptoms: lag, replay, duplicate processing, schema error, or poison event.
Inspect: offsets, consumer group lag, DLQ rate, retry count, and event keys.
During campaigns and product launches, consumer lag spikes badly and downstream freshness
SLAs are missed.
hints: Whether you can diagnose lag as a capacity and design question, not just 'Kafka is slow'.

tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Measure where time is spent - consume, transform, or sink. • Increase partitions only if the pipeline can truly use more parallelism end to end. • Reduce per-record cost with batching, asynchronous writes, or moving heavy enrichment out of the hot path. • Stabilize consumer groups to reduce unnecessary rebalances.
productionExplanation: Lag means the consumers cannot sustainably keep up with incoming throughput. The bottleneck could be processing speed, partitioning, downstream sink speed, or rebalancing overhead. Trade-offs: • More partitions improve scaling but increase operational overhead and potential hot-partition complexity. • Simplifying real-time logic can improve lag but may shift some enrichment to downstream batch layers. Monitoring and testing: • Track lag by partition, records processed per second, rebalance frequency, sink latency, and end-to-end event freshness.
commonMistakes: Too few partitions for the required consumer parallelism.

title: The Exactly-Once Myth
tags: Candidate confuses source offsets with end-to-end exactly once

hints: Whether you understand that exactly-once claims depend on the full pipeline, not just the source consumer.

tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.

modelSolution: • Answer carefully: many pipelines are at-least-once with idempotent sinks, which is often the practical target. • Use deterministic keys, transactional or merge-based sinks, and replay-safe logic so duplicate delivery does not create duplicate business outcomes. • Be precise about what layer provides which guarantee.

modelSolution: • Introduce a bad-record handling path such as dead-letter topics or quarantine tables with full payload and error reason. • Advance healthy records while preserving failed records for investigation and replay. • Classify producer contract violations and push fixes upstream with visibility.

title: The Rebalance Storm
businessContext: A Kafka-based pipeline usually keeps up with demand, but during deployments, instance churn, or sporadic network instability the consumer group begins rebalancing repeatedly.
problemStatement: Freshness collapses even though raw incoming throughput has not changed much, because consumers spend too much time rejoining rather than processing.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Frequent consumer-group rebalances destroy throughput
logs:
[Kafka] The Rebalance Storm
Consumer symptoms: lag, replay, duplicate processing, schema error, or poison event.
Inspect: offsets, consumer group lag, DLQ rate, retry count, and event keys.
Freshness collapses even though raw incoming throughput has not changed much, because
consumers spend too much time rejoining rather than processing.
hints: Whether you can identify rebalances themselves as a throughput killer, not just a side effect.
Consumer instances are scaling up and down too aggressively.
Measure rebalance frequency and duration explicitly before assuming the problem is sink latency or
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Measure rebalance frequency and duration explicitly before assuming the problem is sink latency or transformation cost. • Stabilize the consumer group through calmer scaling, safer deployments, and settings that match processing cadence. • Reduce per-batch processing time or decouple heavy downstream work so consumers can maintain healthy poll behavior. • Where supported, use assignment strategies and deployment patterns that minimize full-group disruption.
productionExplanation: The issue is that lag may be caused by coordination churn rather than slow message processing. If the group is unstable, consumers lose useful work time and may repeatedly revoke partitions mid-flight. Trade-offs: • Fast autoscaling improves elasticity, but excessive churn can cost more than it saves. • Longer processing batches may improve efficiency, though they can make consumer liveness settings harder to tune. • Safer rollout strategies reduce throughput shocks, but they may slow deployment velocity. Monitoring and testing: • Track rebalance count, rebalance duration, lag growth during membership changes, and partition revocation frequency. • Record deployment events alongside lag charts so coordination issues are visible. • Test rolling updates under realistic traffic rather than only in idle periods.
commonMistakes: Consumer instances are scaling up and down too aggressively.
Long processing intervals make heartbeats or poll cadence unhealthy for the configured settings.
Deployments or container restarts are rolling too disruptively.
Topic assignment patterns or client settings cause the group to rebalance more often than the
workload requires.
followUps: What settings influence rebalance behavior?
How do deployments create lag spikes without traffic growth?
When would you change the consumer architecture instead of just tuning configs?

title: The Schema Registry Compatibility Break
tags: Producer schema evolution breaks downstream consumers
businessContext: A Kafka topic uses managed schemas so several consumers can evolve independently as product teams ship changes.
problemStatement: A producer deploys a new schema version that passes its own tests, but one or more consumers fail, mis-parse records, or begin dropping fields after the rollout.
requirement: Diagnose and fix the issue: Producer schema evolution breaks downstream consumers
logs:
[Kafka] The Schema Registry Compatibility Break
Consumer symptoms: lag, replay, duplicate processing, schema error, or poison event.
Inspect: offsets, consumer group lag, DLQ rate, retry count, and event keys.
A producer deploys a new schema version that passes its own tests, but one or more consumers
fail, mis-parse records, or begin dropping fields after the rollout.
hints: Whether you understand that schema evolution needs compatibility rules and consumer-aware rollout discipline.
The producer introduced a non-compatible field change such as rename, required-field addition, or
Define and enforce compatibility policy appropriate to the topic - backward, forward, or full
tasks: Diagnose the issue.
Fix or explain the production-safe solution.
Explain the answer in interview style.
modelSolution: • Define and enforce compatibility policy appropriate to the topic - backward, forward, or full compatibility depending on the consumer pattern. • Require producer changes to be evaluated against actual consumer expectations, not just against a local serialization test. • Treat destructive schema changes as versioned contract changes with migration planning, not as routine deploys. • Build consumers to tolerate additive evolution where that is part of the contract.
productionExplanation: The problem is not just serialization format. Streaming contracts must define what kinds of schema changes are allowed and how consumers are expected to handle them over time. Trade-offs: • Strict compatibility rules protect downstreams, but they can slow producer iteration. • Flexible contracts improve agility, though they demand more discipline from consumer implementations. • Parallel versioned topics or migrations are safer for breaking changes, but they increase operational complexity. Monitoring and testing: • Track schema-change frequency, consumer parse failures, incompatible registration attempts, and time-to-detect downstream contract breaks. • Replay representative historical and new messages through key consumers during schema-change validation. • Maintain topic-level documentation showing allowed evolution patterns and ownership.
commonMistakes: The producer introduced a non-compatible field change such as rename, required-field addition, or
incompatible type update.
Compatibility mode or review process was too weak for the consumer ecosystem.
Consumers assumed a fixed schema instead of coding defensively for expected evolution.
Testing validated the producer schema in isolation, not the downstream compatibility impact.
followUps: What compatibility mode would you choose for a shared topic?
When is a new topic version better than evolving the old one?
